coredata.dc:title: A review on rapid state of health estimation of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown Copyright © 2023 Published by Elsevier Ltd.

Zhao, Xiaoyu
Fu, Lei
Zhen, Dong
Gu, Fengshou
Ball, Andrew D.
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion battery has presented a rapid growth as the power source of electric vehicles (EVs). The state of health (SOH) estimation plays an important role in ensuring the safe operation of the battery system. Currently, the model-based and data-driven methods have been comprehensively reviewed by considering strengths and drawbacks. However, these approaches present high complexity due to the complex test, modelling and processing algorithm. Developing rapid SOH estimation methods based on the simple test can help suppress the estimation cost and improve the estimation efficiency. However, there is no work to review the development of the rapid SOH estimation for EV batteries and the traditional classification needs to be updated to align with the current state of research. This paper reviews and discusses the state-of-the-art of rapid SOH estimation techniques for EV batteries over the past decade. Particularly, it gives the reclassifications and working principles of the current estimation techniques. Moreover, their advantages and disadvantages when applied to the practice are discussed by incorporating experimental studies. Eventually, this paper gives meaningful suggestions on both practical applications and future development of rapid estimation methods. It is considered that this review work can suggest valuable guidance for academic investigation and engineering applications.

head.ce:title: A review on rapid state of health estimation of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion battery has presented a rapid growth as the power source of electric vehicles (EVs). The state of health (SOH) estimation plays an important role in ensuring the safe operation of the battery system. Currently, the model-based and data-driven methods have been comprehensively reviewed by considering strengths and drawbacks. However, these approaches present high complexity due to the complex test, modelling and processing algorithm. Developing rapid SOH estimation methods based on the simple test can help suppress the estimation cost and improve the estimation efficiency. However, there is no work to review the development of the rapid SOH estimation for EV batteries and the traditional classification needs to be updated to align with the current state of research. This paper reviews and discusses the state-of-the-art of rapid SOH estimation techniques for EV batteries over the past decade. Particularly, it gives the reclassifications and working principles of the current estimation techniques. Moreover, their advantages and disadvantages when applied to the practice are discussed by incorporating experimental studies. Eventually, this paper gives meaningful suggestions on both practical applications and future development of rapid estimation methods. It is considered that this review work can suggest valuable guidance for academic investigation and engineering applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Electric vehicle (EV) has been regarded as one of the most popular patterns to address the negative effects from greenhouse gas emissions and the fossil energy crisis for carbon neutrality [1–3]. Recently, the market dominance of EVs has been increasing dramatically to replace traditional transportation [4]. Lithium-ion batteries play a crucial part in the power storage and supply of EVs owing to their special competitive merits, such as long cycle span, high energy density, and low self-discharging rate [5–7]. However, the lithium-ion battery undoubtedly experiences mechanical and chemical degradations due to the cycling mechanism [8,9] and calendar ageing due to random parking, driving, charging and discharging behaviours, and uncertain storage voltage and temperature in real-world applications [10,11], thus reducing the performance of the battery in terms of capacity and power. Additionally, the total amount of EV batteries reaching end-of-life (EoL) will be growing exponentially year after year with the rapid growth of EVs on roads [12,13]. A substantial number of EV batteries will retire from EVs as the faded capacity or power cannot meet the usage of EVs. However, there is still 70%-80% remaining capacity that can be recycled for secondary uses, such as household energy storage systems, electric tourist cars and so on [14,15]. Reprocessing the retired EV batteries include the offline SOH estimation for the classification, disassembling and repacking. Therefore, developing the rapid and simple SOH test and evaluation technology is essential for economical and environmentally friendly applications of EVs in practice. On the one hand, this targeted technology is expected to realize accurate online SOH estimation, which can reduce the estimation complexity and ensure safety and reliability. Moreover, it can achieve the rapid and simple offline evaluation of the battery SOH.

SOH is an essential parameter to delivering the health condition and battery performance. Developing accurate SOH evaluation techniques for EV batteries has been an interesting research hotspot as it still faces large challenges because of its complex electrochemical characteristics. The battery SOH cannot be directly measured by current commercial sensors, while it can only be estimated using the measured parameters, such as charging/discharging current, voltage, sampling time, temperature and so on [16,17]. Traditionally, a variety of methods have been built up for the SOH estimation of EV batteries, including the direct measurement method, differential analysis method, model-based method, and data-driven method [18].

The direct measurement method focuses on the battery capacity test as the percentage of remaining battery capacity to the rated capacity is a typical definition to measure the battery life. It is a stand test to precisely measure the actual battery SOH, while it needs a strict process for both charging and discharging [19,20]. Therefore, this method is time-consuming and generally used for offline applications to calibrate the battery SOH. Additionally, differential analysis (DA) methods, including incremental capacity analysis (ICA) [21,22], differential voltage analysis (DVA) [23], and differential thermal voltammetry (DTV) [24], can be adopted to evaluate the battery SOH. It has been validated that the calculated peak amplitude drops, and the peak position moves with the degradation of the battery capacity [25–27]. However, these tests need long-time measurements throughout the discharging process, and they are generally suitable for the constant current (CC) discharging process.

The model-based and data-driven methods have been widely developed and verified as effective for accurate online SOH evaluation. On the one hand, the model-based methods need to reproduce the dynamic characteristics of the battery, such as the electrical equivalent circuit model (EECM) [28–30] and the electrochemical model (EM) [31,32]. They aim to extract the life-related features for the measurement of the battery SOH. For example, ECM and EM can capture the internal ohmic resistance and the average li-ion concentration for the battery SOH evaluation, respectively. However, these kinds of methods increase the implementation complexity as they require a lot of preliminary experiments, parameter measurements and extensive computations. Especially for EMs, it is not reasonable for them to be used in real-world estimations due to the unmeasurable parameters and complex partial differential calculations [33]. For another thing, data-driven methods focus on the nonlinear mapping between health features and the battery SOH. A variety of deep learning and machine learning approaches, such as improved support vector regression/machine (SVR/SVM) [34,35], backpropagation neural network (BPNN) [36,37], convolutional neural network (CNN) [38–40], long short-term memory (LSTM) [41–44], Gaussian process regression (GPR) [45–47], and hybrid neural network [48–52] have been developed for the online SOH prediction of the battery. However, they tend to be complex owing to a lot of variable measurements and computations in the training and prediction process. Despite the high implementation complexity, model-based methods and data-driven methods can be used for online SOH estimation in practice. However, they cannot be applied offline to the retired batteries for rapid SOH evaluation.

The above SOH estimation methods tend to be conducted based on complex experiments, dynamic modelling, high-quality large volumes of data, and complicated algorithm implementation, which hence causes high complexity and low efficiency for the battery SOH estimation. With the development of the advanced sensing technology and theoretical foundation for the monitoring of the battery state, it is expected to achieve rapid SOH estimation based on the sample test and data processing. This will facilitate the progression of the lithium-ion battery related-industries. To date, a variety of review studies have also been conducted to discuss the state-of-the-art of lithium-ion battery SOH estimation. Lipu et al. [53] and Xiong et al. [54] comprehensively discussed the battery SOH estimation in 2018, especially for the algorithm implementation in model-based and data-driven approaches. Li et al. [55] mainly reviewed the data-driven strategies for battery lifetime prediction in view of their feasibility and cost-effectiveness in practical applications. Wang et al. [56] provided a deep dissection of deep learning networks for the remaining useful life (RUL) forecast of lithium-ion batteries, which indicates the deep convolutional neural network-extreme learning machine algorithm is a good choice for accurate online battery RUL prediction. Ge et al. [57] also classified the battery SOH evaluation technologies and discussed their applications; however, it mainly concentrated on the model-based and data-driven methods. Moreover, Luo et al. [58] reviewed the current machine learning and deep learning methods for SOH prediction of lithium-ion batteries. Manoharan et al. [59] only focused on the current commonly used support vector machine (SVM), gradient boosting and artificial neural network (ANN) for the battery SOH prediction. Ren et al. [60] summarized the typical machine learning methods for SOH estimation in terms of the basic principle and directions for further optimization. Ouyang et al. [61] discussed the Gaussian filters and Bayesian methods for the health diagnosis of the lithium-ion battery and suggested future trends for the application to onboard health diagnosis. Current review studies mainly focus on the review and discussion of the model-based and data-driven approaches, while they do not symmetrically and comprehensively discuss the application prospect of the rapid and simple SOH evaluation techniques of lithium-ion batteries.

Currently, the rapid SOH estimation of EV batteries has become a research hotspot. Therefore, a new SOH estimation classification is proposed according to the current research status. As shown in Fig. 1 , all the methods can be grouped into two categories, including complex methods and rapid methods, according to the implementation complexity in practical applications. In addition to the complex estimation methods, some potential methods that can enable rapid battery SOH estimation have been categorised into two main groups according to their working principle. They include electrical parameter-based estimation methods and material properties-based estimation methods. These methods do not rely on measurements of the specific charging/discharging curve and achieve the SOH estimation at a given test point without considering the historical information of the battery.

The recent review studies cannot sufficiently report the progress of the SOH test and estimation methods of lithium-ion batteries. Motivated by the lack of a systematic review of the rapid SOH evaluation technologies, this paper is dedicated to reviewing and discussing the progress and prospects of rapid and simple SOH estimation methods for lithium-ion batteries, as highlighted in Fig. 1. In consideration of the deficiency of the recent review work, the key contributions of this paper are presented as follows:
1)
The current SOH estimation methods are summarized considering the complexity, and the rapid and simple SOH estimation technologies are classified into two parts: electrical parameters-based estimation methods and material properties-based estimation methods. The first category includes pulse test technique (PTT), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and partial charge (PC) based method, and the second part includes ultrasonic inspection and active acoustic emission (AE) detection.
2)
Based on experimental studies and review, the recent research progress is discussed in view of working principles, applications, advantages, and disadvantages.
3)
The overall comparison among these methods is given, and the recommendations for further development are also provided for reliable applications in the future.

The remainder of the paper is organised as follows: Section 2 gives the definition of the SOH in theory. The rapid SOH estimation technologies are specially discussed in Section 3. Section 4 comprehensively compares these methods and gives valuable suggestions for further improvement and reliable applications in the future. Finally, the obtained conclusions from this work are given in Section 5.

The lithium-ion battery includes four main parts, namely electrolyte, anode, cathode, and separator. Therefore, the performance of the battery relies on the properties of these components. With the increase in cycling, lithium-ion batteries inevitably experience degradation, including mechanical and chemical effects. Mechanical degradation is an essential contribution to power fading, and it generally occurs in the change of the cell shape, such as volume shrinkage or expansion caused by the deintercalation and intercalation of lithium ions during the charging or discharging process. This can introduce stress release and lead to fractures or cracks on both the cathode and anode, thus reducing the performance of the battery [62]. As an electrochemical system, the charging/discharging process can thus result in chemical degradations, including the decomposition of the electrolyte, the formation of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer on the anode surface, etc. These irreversible phenomena can consume electrolytes and active lithium ions, thus causing the capacity degradation and resistance increase of the battery [63,64]. In addition to cycling degradation, the degradation of the battery can be caused by calendar ageing behaviours. Different from cycling ageing, calendar ageing appears in the process when the battery is idle without charging and discharging in which the battery capacity degrades gradually [11]. Batteries will experience long-time calendar ageing between manufacture and the real-world application in the battery system. It is validated that calendar ageing can lead to the loss of both lithium inventory and active materials at the electrode [65]. The degradation process is accompanied by changes in some battery characteristic parameters, including the increase of the internal resistance and the attenuation of the maximum available capacity. Generally, it can be considered that the battery comes to the end of life (EOL) when the battery’s internal resistance is doubled, or the battery capacity degrades to 80% of the nominal capacity [66].

SOH is a significant measure of the level of battery degradation. The SOH detection is able to indicate the RUL of the battery, therefore ensuring reliability and prolonging the battery’s life. It is noted that the battery SOH can be utilised to measure the overall degradation level and alert when the battery should be replaced with a new one. The battery SOH can be characterised by the percentage of the current state to the initial state of the capacity or internal resistance as follows [57,67]:

presents the initial internal ohmic resistance of the new battery with 100% SOH, and

shows the internal ohmic resistance at the EOL of the battery. The internal ohmic resistance can be calculated based on the short-time pulse test and estimated using the commonly used parameter identification method combined with the equivalent circuit model. These two slow-changing characteristic variables can be selected for the definition of the battery SOH. However, it cannot be directly acquired through any commercial sensors in practice. It can only be estimated based on measurements of electrical parameters or mechanical parameters.

As illustrated in Fig. 1, the rapid SOH estimation approaches are divided into electrical parameters-based estimation methods and material properties-based estimation methods in this paper. In particular, the first category includes the pulse test technique (PTT) method and electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) measurement method, and the second category consists of ultrasonic inspection and a proposed active acoustic emission (AE) detection technology. In this section, they are particularly reviewed and discussed regarding their working principles and applications.

As mentioned above, EECM has been developed into an effective duplication of the electrochemical dynamics of lithium-ion batteries. Especially for the first-order RC and the second-order RC models that can be seen in Supplementary Material (Appendix 1), they have become the popular models for battery modelling and then the state estimation [68–71].
U
OCV
indicates the open circuit voltage (OCV) across the battery at rest.
I L
stands for the charging/discharging current.
R 0
is the battery ohmic resistance.
U L
is the terminal voltage of the battery. The parallel connected
R 1
and
C 1
stand for the polarization resistance and polarization capacitance, respectively. The additional RC network can increase the modelling precision but at the cost of the computing complexity.

An increasing number of researchers have dedicated to studying the possible correlation between the degradation degree and model parameters for SOH estimation. For example, Yang et al. [72] utilized the 1RC model to model the battery, and the identified model parameters were used for the model training based on the particle swarm optimization-least square support vector regression (PSO-LSSVR) method. Based on the 2RC model, Tan et al. [73] focused on the identification of the dynamic model parameters and then achieved the battery SOH prediction through the support vector regression (SVR) method. Similarly, Ma et al. [74] utilized the unscented Kalman filter (UKF) to predict the battery SOH based on the EECM. Moreover, Chen et al. [75] investigated the changing law of the increment of the ohmic resistance
R 0
and polarization resistance
R 1
and performed the SOH prediction using a novel metabolic extreme learning machine method. It is found that the equivalent ohmic resistance is an essential indicator for the battery SOH characterization.

Based on the EECM theory, the PTT can be rapidly carried out to recognise the battery’s internal resistance, and therefore the battery SOH can be evaluated according to the mapping correlation between the internal resistance and battery SOH. Fig. 2 illustrates the pulse discharging current and the corresponding voltage response, where the battery terminal voltage experiences a transient drop from
t a
to
t b
caused by the loading current. Thus, the battery’s internal resistance can be calculated by the incremental voltage
Δ U
and current
Δ I
as follows:
(3)
R 0
= Δ U / Δ I

A variety of studies have focused on model parameter identification based on this method [76,77]. The working principle can be explained by the EECM. Taking the 1RC model as an example, Fig. 3 displays the dynamic voltage response in the equivalent circuit caused by the transient pulse current. It is noted that the transient discharging current mainly flows through the ohmic resistance once the pulse current is applied to the battery. Therefore, the battery’s internal resistance can be calculated based on transient increments of the voltage
Δ
U L
and current
Δ
I L
.

It has been validated that the resistance of several types of batteries shows an increasing trend with the battery SOH as displayed in Fig. 4 . As a result, the battery ohmic resistance can be identified and adopted for the battery SOH indicator.

Lin et al. [80] focused on the extraction of the health indicators based on the short pulse response characteristics and achieved high SOH estimation based on an improved fuzzy cerebellar model neural network, which shows that voltage response characteristics in the transient current pulse can effectively characterise the ageing of the battery. Braco et al. [81] extracted the internal resistance as the indicator of the battery SOH from the pulse test. The results revealed that the larger the capacity, the lower the internal resistance. Moreover, Braco et al. [82] incorporated the measurement of both the AC resistance and DC resistance based on the pulse test to indicate the current capacity level for the second-life cell battery, module, and pack. In particular, the built relationship between the measured OCV and SOC can guide the resistance measurement at different SOCs. It is thought that the proposed method can reduce the test time within 2 min for per cell. However, the measurement of both OCV and AC/DC resistance needs to consider the influence of both temperature and SOH. Moreover, the sensitivity of the excitation frequency for AC impedance testing needs to be explored to ensure more efficient AC impedance measurements.

It is believed that the PTT can be used for the rapid internal resistance calculation and then the SOH estimation using the mapping relationship between the internal resistance and degraded capacity. To test the validation of the PTT, an experimental study was also performed based on two cell batteries connected in series. As displayed in Supplementary Material (Appendix 2), the designed battery test rig includes a host computer, a battery tester, a 16-bit data acquisition system, two cell batteries and a temperature chamber. The battery tester is controllable for the charging, discharging and cycle test, in which the battery is put in the temperature chamber at 25℃ for stable testing temperature. The connection of two cell batteries in the circuit can be seen in Supplementary Material (Appendix 3). The targeted battery is a kind of polymer pouch lithium-ion battery, and its crucial specifications are listed in the Supplementary Material (Appendix 4).

In the test, two brand-new batteries were degraded by the cycle test. The PTT was carried out every 20 cycles, and it happens between 100% SOC and 10% SOC every 10% SOC. Finally, the mean internal resistance under different SOCs was used to characterise the battery SOH. It can be found from Fig. 5 , the calculated internal resistance of cell 1 or two cell batteries increases with cycles. In particular, Fig. 5(c) and Fig. 5(d) present the fitting correlation between the mean internal resistance and actual battery SOH based on the first-order polynomial function, respectively. The obtained
R
2
can reach up to 97%, which reveals that the battery’s internal resistance is strongly correlated with the battery SOH.

Further, Table 1 presents the SOH estimation errors using the measured resistance by PTT. The obtained high estimation accuracy in terms of various evaluation metrics, such as
R
2
(R-squared), RMSE (root mean squared error), MAE (mean absolute error), and MAPE (mean absolute percentage error), reveal that the PTT can be a rapid test method for the internal resistance measurement and then battery SOH evaluation.

EIS is an non-invasive and information-rich test technology that focuses on the measurement of the battery impedance [83]. It has been developed into a popular application for the battery SOH estimation. It is easily carried out by imposing a small current or voltage excitation to the battery and collecting the dynamic voltage response. The excitation signal is generally the sinusoidal mode, and the battery impedance can be obtained as expressed as follows [84]:

where I presents the sinusoidal current excitation at frequency f , which causes the voltage response V and phase φ across the battery. Therefore, the whole impedance of the battery can be calculated at each of the specific frequency f . The lithium-ion battery is a complicated electrochemical system. It is noted that the excitation with different currents can lead to different dynamic responses of the battery. Based on various ranges of frequency excitations and Fourier transform, the EIS spectrum can be obtained in Nyquist’s plot that presents the imaginary and real parts [85]. As illustrated in the Supplementary Material (Appendix 5), the EIS spectrum can be modelled through the typical EECM to characterise the electrochemical dynamics [86]. According to different frequency excitation, the EIS arc can be grouped into three parts, including the high-frequency area (>10 kHz), the middle-frequency area (0.1–10 kHz), and the low-frequency area (<0.1 Hz). In the model, the inductance L is used to present the stray inductance caused by the wire and collector. The internal resistance
R 0
stands for the resistance introduced by electrodes, electrolytes, and diaphragm, and its value can be captured in the EIS spectrum when the impedance spectrum passes through the real axis.
Rsei
is used to denote the formation of the SEI film.
C P E 1
means the constant phase element that indicates the film capacitor owing to the dispersion effect. The parallel connected
Rsei
and
C P E 1
are used to present the SEI impedance as shown in the upper part of the mid-frequency area.
Rct
is the charge transfer resistance that is characterised by electrons moving from the electrolyte to electrodes.
C P E 2
indicated the caused double layer capacitor by dispersion effects. Connected with
Rct
, they indicate the interfacial impedance between electrodes and electrolytes, which can better describe the lower part in the mid-frequency area. Additionally,
Z w
presents the straight line in the low-frequency part, which denotes the impedance caused by the lithium ions diffusion process in the solid particles. Thus, the overall battery impedance based on the EIS test can be described as follows:

Compared to traditional estimation approaches based on the electrical parameter measurement, EIS can capture more electrochemical information for the battery SOH evaluation. Many studies have focused on developing the intrinsic relationship between EIS impedance and battery degradation for the SOH evaluation. Zhang et al. [87] have demonstrated the effectiveness of using the whole EIS spectrum for the battery SOH and RUL prediction based on the Gaussian process regression (GPR) method. It is found that the EIS test is significantly affected by two key variables, including temperature and SOC [88,89]. Wang et al. [53] performed the battery SOH prediction considering the influence from both the temperature and SOC. As can be seen from the results shown in Supplementary Material (Appendix 6), the impedance spectra move rightward and hence cause small internal resistance with the increase of the temperature. The impedance spectrum in the mid-frequency part presents large fluctuation with the decrease of SOC. Moreover, it is indicated that the EIS spectra move rightward, the high-frequency region presents a trend of getting smaller and the middle-frequency region shows a reverse conclusion with the degradation.

In addition to the sinusoidal frequency excitation for the EIS operation, Yokoshima et al. [90] have compared the traditional square, triangular, and sawtooth current waves for battery impedance calculations. It is found that the square current-based EIS can achieve the battery impedance measurement with higher accuracy. Moreover, a variety of studies have investigated different excitation frequencies for the EIS impedance measurement and the SOH evaluation, including 0.1 Hz [91], 316 Hz [92], 1 kHz [93], 10 kHz [94], and so on. Especially, Carthy et al. [95] found that the imaginary impedance obtained between 4.8 kHz and 10 kHz of the high-frequency region shows a close correlation with the SOH of the battery while decreases with the decrease of the input frequency. Based on the EIS dataset [87], various components, such as real part, imaginary part, actual impedance and phase, from the EIS spectrum obtained by the excitation frequency of 8.818 Hz are discussed to compare the relationship with battery SOH. Fig. 6 shows the changing trend of battery SOH and different parts from the EIS spectrum. By contrast, both the imaginary part and phase show higher correlations with the battery SOH with
R
2
up to 0.99. Obviously, the imaginary part increases with SOH, while the phase varies reversely. It is concluded that the imaginary part and phase can be used for the rapid battery SOH evaluation at a specific frequency excitation.

Furthermore, the different impedance components are discussed to build the relationship between SOH and the calculated impedance of the battery. Carthy et al. [96] conducted the sensitivity analysis of charge-transfer resistance, internal resistance, and double-layer capacitance variables for battery SOH characterization. As can be seen in Supplementary Material (Appendix 7), it is seen that the internal resistance shows higher sensitivity to the battery SOH. To realize the high estimation accuracy of the battery SOH, Chang et al. [86] incorporated the SEI film resistance and charge transfer resistance obtained in the middle-frequency area for fitting the SOH with the ageing of the battery. Jiang et al. [97] have explored the extraction of different features from the EIS spectrum and compared their mapping relationship with the degraded capacity. As can be seen in Supplementary Material (Appendix 8), the internal resistance
R 0
, SEI film impedance
R 1
and charge transfer impedance
R 2
are strongly correlated to the faded capacity of the battery and present an upward trend with degradation. In contrast, the charge transfer impedance
R 2
is more sensitive to battery degradation. Although the comparative study for effective feature extraction can improve the estimation accuracy, the additional variable identification increases the computing complexity. Instead of the complex model parameter identification, Faraji-Niri et al. [98] directly extracted a variety of typical health indicators from the EIS spectrum based on the simple calculation to perform the accelerated SOH estimation of second-life lithium-ion batteries. The GPR model helps to predict the SOH with good RMSE and MSE low than 1.65%. The authors also highlighted that both the temperature and SOC are two essential factors affecting the SOH estimation accuracy. Moreover, some works have incorporated PTT and EIS measurements for the feature extraction and battery SOH characterization thanks to their strength of being fast. For example, Wang et al. [99] have studied the extraction of the internal resistance and impedance from both PTT and EIS measurements, which focuses on building the correlation between extracted features and battery health state for regrouping the retired batteries. The internal resistance was extracted from the short-time dynamic voltage, and two impedance features were extracted from the EIS spectrum using the sweep frequency ranging from 0.1 Hz to 1000 Hz. The fuzzy clustering algorithm was used to develop the regrouping strategy, and only 6 mins were taken for the classification work. Ramirez-Meyers et al. [100] proposed the quantitative assessment of the battery SOH based on the direct-current internal resistance (DCIR) and EIS test using the excitation frequency between 10 kHz and 50 MHz A linear regression was used to fit the correlation between internal resistance and the remaining capacity with
R
2
= 0.713
, and it was found that 89 Hz can offer a high regression correlation between the capacity and imaginary impedance with
R
2
= 0.907
.

In addition to the battery internal resistance and impedance measurement, the PC-based methods have recently shown much potential for the rapid battery SOH estimation. The effective characteristic parameters can be extracted from the charging profile for indicating the health status of the battery. Compared to the random discharging process, the EV battery often works at stable constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) or multi-step constant current-constant voltage (MCC-CV) charging mode, which makes it easier to extract health-related features [101]. Based on the public Oxford Degradation dataset [102] and NASA dataset [103], the analysed results shown in Supplementary Material (Appendix 9) present the variation of the charging voltage in CC-CV mode under different cycles. It can be seen from the charging voltage that the aged battery needs less time to reach the cut-off voltage, or the terminal voltage increases more quickly. From the charging current in CV mode, the current change rate of the new battery is higher than the aged battery. This is caused by the loss of active lithium ions inside the battery [104]. Therefore, the ageing-related features can be extracted from the partial charging data for modelling the changing trend of the battery SOH.

Many efforts have been made to contribute to battery SOH estimating using charging data. In early studies, some researchers used the complete CC-CV data from NASA dataset as the input data and built the battery SOH prediction model based on GPR [105], least squares support vector machine (LSSVM) [106] and least square support vector regression (LSSVR) [107]. Moreover, the authors [108,109] employed the complete charging data in CC mode for the evaluation of the battery health condition, while the authors [110,111] focused on the charging current curve during the CV stage. In contrast, the usage of the CV stage is more suitable for the real-world battery SOH estimation as the initial charging voltage varies with the uncertain discharging process. However, the feature extraction from the complete CC-CV, CV, and CC processes is time-consuming so that it cannot achieve rapid battery SOH evaluation in realistic conditions.

To overcome the above obstacle for efficient battery SOH estimation, some studies have dedicated to the feature extraction from partial charging segment [101,112–115] instead of the whole CC or CV stage. Wu et al. [112] has attempted to extract the dQdV-related features at the CC voltage range [3.8, 4.1] V and the developed linear regression-recursive feature elimination (LR-RFE) was employed for modelling the battery SOH. Tian et al. [113] also focused on the CC voltage at the range of [3.9, 4.1] V for screening the ageing features. Even though the partial voltage is used for the feature extraction, the health features were analysed and extracted based on the ICA analysis and model parameter identification. On the one hand, the differential analysis is greatly sensitive to the measurement noise, thus leading to inaccurate feature extraction. On the other hand, the noise reduction smoothing of differential analysis and model parameter identification can increase the complexity of the estimator. Therefore, Ruan et al. [114] proposed a CV stage reconstruction method via combining the Q − V modelling and OCV estimation, which extracted the CV capacity as the health indicator for the SOH estimation from the initial 1000-s CV charging data. To make sure the accurate prediction, the initial point of CV phase must be determined accurately. Alternative to complex health feature extraction processes, Ruan et al. [115] directly used the 500-s charging data in the CCCV transition stage as the input feature and achieved the modelling and SOH prediction based on CNN and transfer learning methods. Compared to previous work using 1000-s data, it reduced the amount of data for the feature selection; however, the determination of the transient turning point introduces large challenges for the practical usage. In above studies, the extraction of the high-quality health features is still complex due to the requirement for the availability of sufficient charging data. In order to achieve rapid battery SOH estimation in the practical application, Guo et al. [101] consider the analysis and optimization of partial CV charging segment for sparse feature selection. Three health indicators, including current evolution constant, charging time and charging capacity, are finally selected from the CV process when the current decrease from 0.7C to 0.2C. The proposed health features combined with GPR model can provide satisfactory predictions with 2.9% error under the sparse sampling of 10 s.

To compare the above studies based on the partial charging, a table as shown in Supplementary Material (Appendix 10) presents the target PC segment, health indicators, methods and evaluation accuracy. Overall, the PC-based method has potentials to achieve the rapid battery SOH estimation in offline or real-world applications. However, this strategy requires the prediction appears in the specific CV phase, and hence the incomplete data can affect the PC-based battery SOH estimation.

The PTT and EIS methods mentioned above focus on the internal resistance or impedance calculation based on measurements of voltage and current. In contrast, the material properties-based estimation methods specifically consider the variation of mechanical properties of the battery material that can be regarded as the function of the battery SOH.

Ultrasonic inspection technology occupies an important position in various non-destructive methods, and it has been widely utilised for the recognition of material structure and property [116]. Ultrasonic inspection is performed by introducing the high-frequency sound wave into the material for the characterisation of flaws or property changes. The propagation velocity of the ultrasonic wave depends on the material properties, as shown in Eq.(8) [117].
(8)
v =
K +
4 3
G
ρ
where v stands for the propagation velocity of the ultrasonic wave. K and G present the volume and shear modulus, respectively. ρ denotes the mass density of the battery material. The mechanical waves are propagated into the material and attenuated due to the different acoustic impedances R , as shown in Eq. (9) [118].
(9)
R =
Z 2
-
Z 1
Z 2
+
Z 1
where
Z 2
and
Z 1
present the acoustic impedances of the material with different properties. Therefore, the reflected wave can be analysed to obtain health indicators that can characterise the change in material property and structural integrity. The ultrasonic inspection technique is a promising method for the material structure as the sound wave transmission highly relies on the density and elastic wave of the medium [119].

The ultrasonic system generally includes the ultrasonic generation device, transducers, receiving device and cables. The ultrasonic inspection typically consists of two modes according to the testability of the media interface, including the pulse-echo technique and the through-transmission technique [120]. Fig. 7 illustrates the applications of two detection methods. In terms of the pulse-echo technique, only one transducer is located on one side of the test object, and it is responsible for both high-frequency wave transmission and receiving. It can be applied to the detection where only one side of the media is accessible. In the case of the test object where two sides are allowed for transducer installation, the through-transmission technique, as illustrated in Fig. 7(b), can be used for material detections. It consists of a transmission transducer installed on one side and another transducer for sound wave receiving installed on the opposite side of the test object.

In terms of the ultrasonic propagation in the battery, it can be explained based on the ultrasonic wave theory in solids or liquids. Ladpli et al. [121] utilized the three-dimensional governing motion equations to describe the ultrasonic wave in the battery, as shown in Eq. (10).
(10)
ρ
∂
2
u
∂
t
2
λ + μ
∇
∇ ∙ u
+ μ
∇
2
u
where ρ stands for the density of the battery material, and u indicates the ultrasonic displacement in the three-dimensional coordinates. λ and μ present Lamé’s stiffness constants that can be calculated by Poisson's ratio and Young's modulus. However, the ultrasonic propagation in the battery is complex as the battery is a multi-layered structure and the ultrasound will be reflected, interfered with, and absorbed. Therefore, a variety of ultrasonic activities should be considered to improve the ultrasonic equation of motion for an accurate description of ultrasonic propagation. Moreover, the finite element model (FEM) simulation can be applied to explain the process of ultrasonic transmission in the battery. Li et al. [122] conducted the fluid–solid coupling-based air-coupled ultrasonic FEM simulation for the pouch lithium battery, as shown in Supplementary Material (Appendix 11). From the simulation results, it can be seen that the ultrasound is transmitted in the longitudinal wave, and the change in the material property of the layered structure will greatly influence the propagation of the ultrasonic wave inside the battery.

The irreversible changes of the battery material with ageing can cause different properties, such as density and elastic modulus [123,124]. The ultrasonic inspection technology can be performed easily, and hence it can conduct rapid condition monitoring based on the received acoustic wave. Both pulse-echo and through-transmission techniques can be applied to identify the condition of the lithium-ion battery, which can be achieved indirectly by capturing the change in the battery material property. A lot of works have demonstrated the feasibility of using ultrasonic probing for the battery SOC estimation, in which the time of flight (ToF) and signal amplitude (SA) can respond well to SOC characterization [123,125–127]. As shown in the Supplementary Material (Appendix 12), SA means the maximum signal amplitude of the echo wave, and ToF presents the transmission duration of the sound wave before it is received by the receiver.

Moreover, a variety of researchers have investigated the ultrasonic inspection method for SOH evaluation of different types of lithium-ion batteries, as presented in Table 2 . It is found that different ultrasonic techniques combined with various pulse frequencies of the transducer are employed to monitor the lifespan of the battery. For example, Davies et al. [117] used the through-transmission technique to test the battery at different cycles, and the SVR was used predict the battery SOH based on the measure of ToF with the average error of 1%. Ladpli et al. [121] applied the ultrasonic guided wave for the first time to sense the condition of the battery due to its advantages, such as long-distance transmission and low energy loss. As shown in the Supplementary Material (Appendix 13), four built-in piezoelectric disc transducers are glued on the surface of the pouch battery in which one of the piezoelectric discs serves as the actuator, and the other three can be used for the echo wave receiving. The design and installation of this small sensor can facilitate the application for practical detection. It is found that ToF gradually moves towards a small value and SA shifts to a large value with the ageing of the battery, which reveals that these two indicators can be good measures for battery SOH due to their high correlation. Finally, both SA and ToF were adopted to characterise the battery SOH based on a statistical generalized additive model, showing satisfactory predictions with high deviance explained and adjusted R2 close to 100% and 1, respectively.

Wu et al. [128] conducted the ultrasound-based SOH estimation of LiCoO2 batteries with two capacities. It is concluded that the ToF is more closely related to the change of battery SOH than the indicator of signal amplitude. As shown in the Supplementary Material (Appendix 14), the experimental results of two batteries indicate the ToF is fitted well with the battery SOH. Thus, it can be used to evaluate the battery SOH based on the simple test.

Moreover, Kim et al. [129] designed the pulse-echo monitoring system and estimated the battery SOH using the ToF and SA extracted from the first reflected echo wave. As illustrated in the Supplementary Material (Appendix 15), the ToF shows a rising trend with the increasing number of cycles, and the opposite changing trend occurs on SA. It can be explained by the fact that the velocity of the sound wave reduces, and the attenuation increases due to the elastic softening of the battery material caused by the long-term repeated charging/discharging cycles.

Zhao et al. [130] developed the ultrasonic test rig and explored SOH monitoring based on the direct guided wave. As shown in the Supplementary Material (Appendix 16), two PZTs (10-mm diameter and 1 mm thick) are installed on the same side of the battery surface at 90 mm, in which one PZT is used for the excitation source, and another is served for receiving the signal response. The results, as shown in the Supplementary Material (Appendix 17), displays changes in SA and ToF obtained by various excitation frequencies to cycles. It shows that both battery SOC and the excitation frequency can significantly affect the test results. A consistent trend can be received with cycles based on direct guided wave detection, and it is that both SA and ToF decrease with cycles. However, the detecting sensitivity shows high before 60 cycles, and the variation gradually reduces with increasing cycles.

In summary, it is validated that the SA and ToF are two effective estimators for indicating the battery SOH from the perspective of ultrasonic detection. This method can be operated simply to achieve rapid battery SOH evaluation. However, the estimation accuracy is easily affected by various factors, such as the excitation frequency, SOC and even temperature. Additionally, the only detection parameters, including SA and ToF, are not sufficient for accurate battery SOH estimation.

In summary, the ultrasonic inspection technique is feasible for the rapid battery SOH evaluation but lacks robustness for accurate and reliable SOH monitoring. For example, the limited characteristic parameters, such as ToF and SA, extracted from the single frequency pulse are insufficient to capture the variation in the mechanical property of the battery, thus causing incorrect SOH estimation. Moreover, the detecting ultrasound has the drawback of low energy and a small detection area, which makes it difficult to ensure accurate SOH evaluation due to the complicated battery structure.

To solve these problems, we hence propose an active AE sensing technique for the rapid and robust battery SOH evaluation [131,132]. Fig. 8 illustrates the mechanism of the developed active AE technique. The proposed approach combines the application of both power ultrasound and AE methods. First of all, continuous longitudinal ultrasound with appropriate power is employed to stimulate the battery. During the transmission of ultrasound, ultrasonic transmission, ultrasonic reflection, ultrasonic interference, and ultrasonic absorption will occur, resulting in the generation and transmission of AE signals from the battery material. According to the constitutive properties of the material and the acoustic wave transfer, the active AE signal can be viewed as a function of the mechanical properties of the battery material, such as Young's modulus E, Poisson's ratio υ, and density ρ. On the other hand, the change of electrode mechanical properties caused by battery degradation will interfere with the transmission of the ultrasound in the battery and affect the change of material AE signals. The broadband AE sensor is used to receive active AE signals, and effective features can be extracted through effective signal analysis methods to establish the mapping with the battery health status, which enables fast and accurate battery SOH evaluation.

In contrast to conventional detecting ultrasound, continuous power ultrasound can provide higher signal energy for the stable transmission of the ultrasonic wave inside the battery [133,134]. As mentioned previously, the mechanical property of the battery material change with ageing due to the irreversible fading of the battery. It is believed that the appropriate power ultrasound input can excite different responses at the micro level under different SOHs. On the other hand, AE is characterized by elastic waves released by the material owing to changes in the mechanical material property [135]. AE is a non-destructive detection technique for the structural integrity of the material as it is able to collect the high-frequency elastic wave released by the material. Moreover, AE detection can convert the single-frequency pulse to a wideband signal with multiple frequencies. For example, the AE spectrum consists of a broad range of frequency contents, including the fundamental excitation frequency, harmonics, sub-harmonics, ultra-harmonics, and broadband signal [136,137]. Therefore, the AE technique is adopted to actively sense the produced elastic wave excited by the power ultrasound and sufficient information related to the battery state can support the effective SOH estimation.

To validate the performance of the proposed active AE sensing technology for fast and robust battery SOH estimation, the experimental system is specifically set up to perform the battery SOH detection test. The schematic diagram, as shown in Supplementary Material (Appendix 18), illustrates the developed experimental bench for the active AE sensing test. The vibrator mounted on one side of the battery is stimulated by the power ultrasound generator with a high-frequency pulse of 40 kHz. The acoustic wave is transmitted into the battery, and the AE transducer mounted on the opposing side of the battery can perceive the released elastic wave. Moreover, the battery tester is employed to perform the battery test, including charging, discharging, and cycling for the degradation.

In the test, the tested pouch battery is put in the temperature box to get a stable operating condition at 25 °C. The main specification of the tested battery is listed in Supplementary Material (Appendix 4). As shown in Supplementary Material (Appendix 19), the battery is degraded using repeated charging/discharging tests and the SOH is reduced to around 96.5% after 120 cycles. In particular, the battery state is tested under different levels of SOCs through the power ultrasound excitation and AE signal measurement. The experimental results, as shown in Supplementary Material (Appendix 20), compare the AE waveforms and corresponding spectra under two different cycles. Complex time-domain waveforms and spectra can provide rich information for characterising the battery SOH. For instance, compared with the initial SOH of the battery, the amplitude of the time domain waveform is reduced after 20 cycles. Using the fast Fourier transform (FFT) analysis, a variety of frequency components can be obtained for the battery SOH identification, including

f 0
,

,

,

, and

(
f 0
is the fundamental excitation frequency and
f 0
= 40 k H z
). These different frequency components respond to the different SOH of the battery, which thus provides sufficient information for distinguishing the battery SOH. In particular, the statistical feature RMS is used to analyse the response of the AE signal obtained at different SOCs to the battery state, as shown in Supplementary Material (Appendix 21). Obviously, it is found that the RMS gradually decreases with the decreasing battery SOH. Moreover, it is clear that the RMS presents a relatively linear changing relationship with the battery SOH, showing high R2 coefficient up to 0.986. Therefore, it is validated that the developed active AE sensing method is effective for the battery SOH estimation by considering the change of the battery material property with ageing.

Therefore, it is thought that the developed active AE sensing technique can capture more battery life-related information and effectively tell the difference between battery SOHs based on the simple test and RMS analysis.

As reviewed and discussed above, these four methods have proven effective for rapid battery SOH evaluation based on simple operations. However, they should be used carefully according to the application scenario, and there are still some issues that need to be addressed to meet the smooth application in practice.
As shown in Fig. 9 , it compares these four simple SOH estimation methods from different perspectives in consideration into the practical application.

First of all, PTT can be used for the battery SOH estimation from cell battery level to module/pack level based on the electrical parameter measurements, such as terminal voltage, loading current and temperature. The immediate current pulse can achieve the rapid internal resistance measurement and then the SOH evaluation. Similarly, the EIS measurement method can be applied to both the cell battery and module/pack level based on the impedance measurement through the small current excitation and dynamic voltage response collection. For the PC-based methods, it theoretically can also be applied to battery cell or battery module/pack based on the measured charging data in CC or CV charging modes, including voltage, current and time. For above three methods, the estimation precision can be impacted by the inconsistency among battery cells in the module/pack as the health feature varies with different battery conditions. In contrast, both the ultrasonic inspection method and active AE sensing method can only be validated experimentally at the cell battery level.

In terms of the application mode, it is thought that both PTT and EIS measurement methods can only be used for the offline test and SOH estimation. It is because PTT requires a strict pulse test that cannot be achieved on the real-world lithium-ion battery on the EVs, and the EIS measurement needs additional small current excitation. For example, the lithium-ion battery in EVs generally works in dynamic conditions due to the frequent switching between charging and discharging. In contrast, the PC-based methods can be utilised in both online and offline battery SOH estimations; however, it can only be used in the charging phase when considering the online applications. Additionally, both the ultrasonic inspection method and active AE sensing method focus on acoustic sensing based on ultrasonic excitation, which does not interfere with the normal working conditions of the EV battery. Therefore, they can be conducted in online and offline mode.

Furthermore, both PTT and EIS measurement methods include only one indicator for the battery SOH estimation as they aim at the internal resistance and impedance measurement, respectively. As for PC-based methods, more health indicators can be extracted or reconstructed from partial charging voltages/currents, and the regression model and machine learning methods can help to build the mapping correlation between battery features and SOH. To some extent, the estimation accuracy will depend on the quality of the collected charging data. Generally, the only high-frequency pulse measurement based on the ultrasonic transducer greatly reduces the estimation complexity and cost. However, the ultrasonic inspection method cannot sufficiently reflect the electrochemical dynamics based on the single-frequency pulse excitation and receiving. The only parameters, including ToF and SA, cannot guarantee accurate battery SOH characterization. In contrast, the developed active AE sensing technology is developed to further improve the detection accuracy of the conventional ultrasound-based technique for a more robust and accurate battery SOH evaluation. It is noted that the developed active AE sensing technology can more sufficiently sense the change of mechanical properties with ageing, and it can also provide more health features as they can be extracted from both the complex time-domain and frequency-domain features. It is believed that more features can guarantee more accurate SOH estimation.

These methods have much potential for rapid EV battery SOH estimation in online or offline mode. To guarantee accurate SOH estimation for lithium-ion batteries in practical applications, the challenges and future work are given for further improvement, as shown in Fig. 10 .
(1) Influence of various factors

The lithium-ion battery is a dynamic electrochemical system, and hence these rapid test and estimation methods are significantly affected by various factors, such as temperature, SOC and charging rates, when used in the real systems. For example, temperature is an important influencing factor that will affect the activity of lithium ions and the rate of electrochemical reactions. This can change the mechanical properties of the battery material, such as the anode and cathode. On the one hand, this can affect the propagation of the ultrasonic wave inside the battery and hence cause different measurements of the ultrasonic echo signal. On the other hand, the internal resistance and impedance measured by PIT and EIS methods will be different under different temperatures. The charging profile will vary with the temperature, thus affecting the PC-based methods for the battery SOH evaluation. To obtain a consistent health indicator measurement and accurate SOH estimation, the operations of these methods should ensure the tested battery is placed in a relatively stable temperature environment. Meantime, the different SOC can change the condition of the battery in terms of internal resistance, impedance and mechanical properties of the battery material. The SOH evaluation should be compared at the same SOC level. Moreover, the charging rate will influence the charging profile, and hence the PC-based methods need to be used in the same level of charging rate or by considering the impact from the different charging rates. Overall, these methods should be carefully used in practical applications to enable accurate tests and SOH estimation.
(2) Health indicator extraction

For both PTT and EIS measurement method, the battery internal resistance and impedance have been the commonly used health indictors. As for the PC-based methods, the health indicators need to be obtained from the charging voltage/current. It can work in the charging phase for online/offline battery SOH evaluation. However, the random charging behaviours and initial battery condition bring a huge challenge for the real-world application. Therefore, the different PC segments need to be comprehensively considered to adapt to the practical applications. The effective extraction of sparse health features for different PC segments can help reduce the estimation complexity and improve the estimation accuracy. Different from the PTT, EIS measurement and PC-based methods, both the ultrasound inspection and active AE sensing method give into changes in mechanical material properties for the battery SOH estimation. Currently, the traditional ultrasonic inspection-based methods only focus on the health feature extraction for SA and TOF in the time domain. However, the frequency component and amplitude in the frequency domain perhaps correspondingly change with the degradation of the battery. As for the active AE sensing technology, it has been validated that the time-domain RMS parameters can characterise the battery SOH. However, the propagation mechanism of the power ultrasound inside the battery should be studied to explain the change of the active AE signal with the ageing of the battery. Therefore, more effective health indicators from both the time domain and frequency domain signals can be extracted to estimate the battery SOH more accurately.
(3) Fitting model development

After getting health indicators by these methods under different levels of degradation, it is highly necessary to build the relationship between obtained health indicators and the battery SOH. Generally, the health indicator shows a high linear relationship with the battery SOH, while it still presents fluctuations under some detection points as the battery is a highly nonlinear dynamic system. To ensure accurate SOH estimation in the whole battery lifecycle, it is essential to develop the estimation model based on the nonlinear fitting and even the machine learning methods. In addition, the developed model should consider the influence of various factors mentioned above.
(4) Sensitivity analysis for excitation frequency/wave

In terms of the EIS measurement, ultrasonic inspection, and active AE sensing methods, they are carried out based on the external current or ultrasonic excitation. Currently, a variety of excitation frequencies/waves have been studied for mapping the relationship between extracted health features and the battery SOH. Especially, the measurement of the EIS spectrum requires the current/voltage excitation over a series of frequencies, which undoubtedly increases the estimation complexity compared to the PTT. It has already been demonstrated that the different frequency regions respond differently to the battery SOH. Therefore, the sensitivity analysis of the input frequency is necessary to avoid the additional excitation of the input frequency and then reduce the computing burden. Moreover, the optimal excitation frequency and wave should be explored to discover which frequency range can characterise the battery state based on the received ultrasonic wave or active AE signal, which can guarantee the extraction of the optimal health features and then the accurate EV battery SOH estimation.
(5) Device/hardware development

Both ultrasonic inspection and active AE sensing methods are still in the research and development stage, while they have been demonstrated experimentally effective for the rapid SOH estimation of the cell battery with the transducer installed on the surface of the lithium-ion pouch battery based on the short-period ultrasonic excitation in the lab. Their operations do not interfere with the normal working condition of the lithium-ion battery, and therefore they can be used for online and offline measurements for SOH estimation even though the battery is being charged or discharged. However, the installation of the sensing device is a key issue when it comes to practical applications, especially for the onboard EV battery module/pack SOH estimation. As a result, the sensing device is required to be developed at the micro level so that it can be embedded on the surface of the cell battery in the battery module and pack for the stable data collection.

In this paper, the rapid SOH estimation methods for EV batteries are reviewed and discussed. Considering the operating complexity in practical applications, the current SOH estimation methods are classified into complex and rapid approaches. Specifically, the rapid estimation approaches are grouped into electrical parameter measurement-based estimation methods (PTT and EIS measurement) and material properties-based estimation methods (ultrasonic inspection and active AE sensing). These rapid SOH estimation methods are evaluated by combining with the review and experimental studies. Their working principles, advantages and disadvantages are discussed in consideration for the practical application. Finally, the key challenges used in the real-world application and future work are proposed for further improvement. In summary, both PTT and EIS measurement technologies can only be employed for the offline SOH estimation due to requirements for special test procedures that generally cannot be conducted during the normal operation of the battery. In addition to the single-cell level, they can theoretically be applied to both the battery module and battery pack levels, while the performance should be validated based on the experimental studies, and modelling analysis. In contrast, both ultrasonic inspection and active AE sensing technologies show much potential for the non-destructive testing that will not affect the normal operation of the battery. It is considered that the analysis of the transmission mechanism of ultrasound in the battery will help to improve the accuracy of SOH estimation, including the selection of ultrasound parameters and the extraction of influential health features. Most importantly, the development of smart and micro-sensors can help popularize battery monitoring at the module and pack levels. It is validated that these four approaches can be adopted for the rapid SOH estimation with simple operations. Different methods can be carefully selected for the battery SOH estimation according to the application background.

Zuolu Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiaoyu Zhao: Investigation, Validation. Lei Fu: Investigation. Dong Zhen: Writing – review & editing. Fengshou Gu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision. Andrew D. Ball: Writing – review & editing.
